sentences.text: And her mother had remained in the insane asylum in Dijon.
And before Rita left, I told her, Rita, I'm going to go see your mother when I go back to Dijon for school.
I'm going to go visiting her.
And Rita says-- and she heard me.
She said don't bother.
We have many friends.
You don't have to go to see my mother.
And I was hurt.
But I went back to Dijon, and me being me at the time of the-- their Reformation, the last Sunday of October, I thought of her.
And I said, I'm going to go see Rita's mother.
So I took my bike again, my good old bike.
The tires were like onion skins.
I was always having flat tires.
And I went to the [INAUDIBLE], as it was called.
And that I saw the sister.
The sister was a young woman from Belgium.
She had the face of an angel.
She was so good, so nice.
And I said, I come to see Madame Thalmann.
Oh, she said, that's nice.
I said, she had many visitors?
She said no, you are the first one.
I couldn't believe it.
I said, you mean nobody came to see her since last spring?
She said, no.
You are the first one.
So after that, I went to see her every Saturday when I didn't have school.
And Rita's mother was insane.
She spoke in Switzerdeutsch, which I did not understand, and Yiddish, in which I made huge progress, which helped me a lot when I came to America.
And German, of course.
And she mistook me for her daughter.
And we would walk in that cold park in the winter.
She had this thin coat.
And she was so skinny, holding her was like holding a bird, you know.
And she was pressing me against her and raving and ranting in German.
And always hungry.
And we had no food.
My mother got some corn meal and she made corn cakes to bring to Madam Thalmann.
But you know they are frittering away.
They don't stay in slices.
And sometimes the crumbs would fall on the floor.
That's why I'm telling you, I don't want Rita to hear that.
And she would go on her hand and knees and eat off the floor like a dog.
And she was a woman who had been distinction incarnate.
You know, it just broke my heart.
I, I couldn't stand it.
But anyway, I went to see her all that winter.
The sisters.
The sisters.
So it was a Catholic--
It was Catholic.
Oh, yes.
And the law-- surely you have heard it many times-- that was anybody who is crazy wasn't supposed to be fed.
They were supposed to die.
So they had no food coupon.
So the nuns had, but how many nuns were there?
Maybe 15.
And they had maybe 100 patients.
What could they eat?
You know, grass, vegetables, things that's were-- and so Madame Thalmann was dying of hunger.
So what I could bring her that could help?
Besides, she was mad.
So one day I came on a Saturday, and she said the nun said to me, Madame Thalmann is ill.
You go to see her but don't kiss her because she must have pneumonia.
And they had taken her in a separate room, and they had put on a radio with Swiss songs to, to help her, you know.
We have a beautiful folklore of Swiss songs.
I mean, the one I learned in school because we were living just that close to border.
And she was there, very hot, very hot.
And I knew she was going to die.
And so I came back two days later, because, you know, they couldn't-- we had no telephone.
And the, the, the sister told me, well, she died last night.
She said, do you want to go see in the morgue which one she is?
I never seen dead people.
I had to go in that morgue and lift all the, the sheets until I found her.
And then we had to organize the burial.
My parents for some reason were not there, and I was the only one.
And the Jewish cemetery was closed, and the Catholic cemetery would not receive anybody not Catholic.
And the Protestants never were really buried.
We were cremated, you know.
So I had to arrange that.
My pastor helped me.
She was in the potter's field in an unmarked grave, but we managed to have it marked, feeling that after the war, her daughter could do that, you know, and it was done.
But anyway, it's like in a French movie or the [FRENCH].
I remember that month of February, when I followed that.
I don't know what you would see her, I suppose, it was an open hearse with a casket.
I guess it's OK for the orthodox Jews, just wood.
You know, making noise on the pavement, with a horse that the Germans even didn't want.
They knew the shape of the horse was all bones.
And the guy was, you know, driving the horse, and me walking behind with the snow falling, and all the bells tolling because everybody was dying.
All the old people were dying for lack of food, lack of heat, lack of everything.
And all the people coming across, they were doing the sign of the cross, because they were Catholic.
And she was Jewish.
I was Protestant.
And all those people signed themselves.
I was very moved, to tell you the truth.
And I walked, and I walked.
Was no other way to go, and the cemeteries are always outside of the cities.

And we crossed a railroad where an old man-- I thought was old, I didn't look at him very much-- was checking the tracks to see that the screws had not been unbolted, as they were all the time by the undergrounds so the, the train would derail.
So he was checking all that.
And he had a pickaxe.
He was hitting to see the-- hear the sound.
And he saw me pass.
I didn't pay much attention to him.
And he threw his pickaxe into the bushes, and he followed me, oh, maybe four, five feet behind me on my right.
But I didn't look.
I just looked from the side.
I could see him walk.
He had one of those caps.
He was dressed in blues, you know, like a very lowly laborer.
And finally we arrived to the potter's field, which was like a football field.
No tree, nothing.
And graves were already dug, because it was the winter, and the ground is to hard to dig.
And the-- a man was there.
And they took the coffin, lowered it into the grave.
And I turned around to thank the man, gone.
I cannot understand how he disappeared.
To this day I wonder, because he was there just a few seconds before, and that field was so big that he couldn't go away without me seeing him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to me it's a mystery.
I still to this day believe it's an angel.
Yeah.
No.
I don't know.
It was unmarked.
The employees.
I mean, just old men who were doing that for a living.
There was no ceremony, no nothing.
And it was just a little number we put in the back.
And then through the Red Cross, I wrote to, to Rita that her mother had died, and that I had been with her and all that.
And all she could answer me, she was still very unorthodox.
She said, don't put any flowers on her grave.
She didn't say thank you.
She said don't put any flowers on her grave.
Well, it was February.
I wasn't going to put any flowers.
Besides, as a Protestant, to me the graves mean nothing.
A cemetery is nothing.
It's nothing, really.
It's just like the, the skin of a of a snake that has gone out of skin.
I mean, it's a shell.
It's an egg shell.
So I never told Rita that she hurt my feelings like that, you know.
I never did.
But I can tell you that in my life, that day when I buried her mother was the saddest day of my life.
I lost my husband, father, mother.
It was not that I loved her mother that much.
I mean, I loved her well enough.
But it was the misery of the world, that kind of concentrated there.
And I was reciting in my head all the psalms, the 23rd psalm of course, and all the other ones that I knew.
The 139's, all the ones I had learned in Sunday school.
And I felt that God had abandoned us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm sure that many of the Jews in the camps felt the same way, because the psalms only speak of protection, salvation, liberation, God is just, he will save us.
And he didn't.
And to this day I wonder.
Well, my, my mother.
My dad, my pastor, my sister.
No, I never spoke about it with anybody.
That was it.
That was my secret.
They didn't care.
She was a Jewish crazy woman.
They didn't care.
They didn't care.
But after the war, Rita come back.
And that I tell you, it's the lowest time.
I didn't think of killing myself, but almost.
Yes, a lot.
I mean, they were living with us.
And I was always constantly used for favor, you know, to help someone who was in jail, to let packages go by.
That I did, but not Jewish, I mean anybody, you know, when I had a fellow student who was put in jail for foolish reason, but anyway he was.
And his mother came.
She said, can you help us to bring packages?
We are being refused.
So I would go, and I would tell a story, that it was a special holiday in our church, and that we had to do things, and-- I didn't say it was a mitzvah, but almost something like that.
And they would let me in.
I don't know why.
But I brought cookies, eclairs, markers, black market stuff.
Somehow I did it.
Because maybe I seemed so innocent and so sure of myself.
How helpful was your church in terms of helping Jews?
Not much.
Was a very small church.
And if they were, they didn't speak about it.
They were probably linked with the [FRENCH], and many were half-Swiss.
So I think there there were links.
I'm sure of it.
But otherwise, we just didn't talk about it.
That was the key.
You have seen in the [FRENCH] people didn't know what other people were really doing.
Then didn't ask questions.
This way nobody could give away anybody, because you didn't know.
And then-- well, then we had the liberation, D-day, the Germans left.
Yeah.
I didn't remember-- D-day I remember as a happy day.
But the liberation, no.
It was--
D-day was the landing of the--
Well, D-day was the 6th of June, but the liberation of Dijon was in August.
Was much later.
And there was a lot of petty vengeance.
Women who had been with Germans were taken out of the hospital with their heads shaved.
They had just given birth.
They had blood all over their, their nightgown, and they had-- holding little babies, and people jeered.
And I hated it.
And they were lynching of the, the wrong people, if you can ever be lynching of the right people.
But they went to arrest people who had nothing to do with it, and they were hung at lampposts.
It was ugly.
No.
No.
No, no.
Oh, it's a crazy story.
The, the Germans left.
And they were, were living in a big avenue, the avenue going to Paris.
And they were leaving.
It was the evening.
They told us, open your windows, take down all your pictures and your mirrors because we are going to blow up the station.
OK.
So we did that.
And the station blew up.
It was strange.
It was like an elephant jumping up in the air.
The-- Dijon.
It was one of those big stations, you know, where they were made with metal things.
You know, the construction of the late 19th century.
And then the Germans left.
And from one, you know, every, I don't know, 100 yards, they would say, nicht verteidigen.
Do not defend.
Do not defend.
Do not defend the city.
So they left.
The next day, nothing.
The following day, the flag of the free French floating, and all the horrors started.
And you know, it's always the people who have suffered least that are the most savaged, because the-- you know, the Jews who came back, they didn't seek vengeance.
They didn't.
They were-- I wouldn't say they were understanding, but they were not bloodthirsty at all.
But the people who had been in the underground for maybe three months, the kind that were colonels, you know, and General DeGaulle came to them and say, oh, see, colonel.
I know-- you see you know how to sew, meaning he could sew his decoration.
Oh, he was mean, General DeGaulle.
He really cut them down to size.
They were those people who, who were very bad.
And the ones who were the first one to go to sleep with German women, I suppose, when they went to Germany.
And the fourth day, the Americans are coming.
So then we went to see.
And lo and behold, what did I see?
A black-- a black regiment coming with music from dixieland, and just prancing around with the rubber shoes, you know, rubber.
And so happy, and smiling, all white teeth.
And they are the ones who really saved us from those Germans with their boots, and all this pom-pom, and those marches.
We couldn't believe it.
We thought it was a musical.
And it was wonderful.
So my husband was put into the apartment where the German had been.
He came after.
He was in the Signal Corps with, with other friend.
And he didn't speak any French, but he spoke German because his wife-- his mother was Viennese.
They were Jews from New York.
And when we saw him the first time, my sister and I, we laughed.
And we said, you look just like [FRENCH].
You know [FRENCH]?
You see the film of propaganda where the horrible [FRENCH] was seen using, raping all the good German women, and he was hung at the end?
Well, my husband looked just like [FRENCH], so I married him.
[LAUGHTER] Just an aside, when the Germans were there, were you getting German propaganda?
Were you going to German movies like [FRENCH]?
Well, occasionally, I did go to German movies to see what was going on, because their news reel showed the reality.
Not of the camps, but of the, the war in Russia.
It was an abomination.
An abomination.
They showed because a soldier who, who looked at it, they couldn't see a Hollywood version.
They knew it wasn't like that.
They came from there.
But it was really horrible.
It was, you know, just the mass killing and everything, horrible.
But otherwise, no.
I mean, they, they, they had their newspaper.
But the French papers were just as bad.
They were translated from the German papers.
And when I went to church on Sunday, it was at 10 o'clock.
The Germans had their service at 9:00.
So they left by the time we arrived.
And on the-- they left, you know, their papers on the-- you know, as you would leave on the, on the-- it was not pews.
They were chairs.
I'm sorry I didn't keep them.
Because it was written that, that Hitler was the new incarnation of Christ.
Yes.
The Germans.
It was their service.
None.
Very little.
Because people were simply not opening their houses.
They still don't.
Very little.
They were all for Petain until the end, where they turned for DeGaulle, of course.
Oh, yes.
If they didn't collaborate, they didn't fight the Germans.
They were with Petain.
When Petain came, the whole city was there, waving flags and-- what's it called?
He had a slogan, travail, famille, patrie, work, family, fatherland.
They were not.
At all.
No.
No, we just never talk about it.
Nobody talked about anything.
Even the people for Petain didn't talk about it.
No, no.
No.
The French didn't put flags on their windows.
I don't think the German even would have allowed French flags then.
Well, we-- I made an American flag.
Did you ever try to make an American flag?
It's very difficult.
We had to dye all sheets with blue ink, with red ink, cut out stars.
Were 48 only at the time, but still it was a job.
But we made it.
Took us a good two days, but we made it.
I.
And my-- and we made the French flag, of course, and we put the cross of-- the double cross of DeGaulle.
But if-- that's how it was.
I didn't meet very many.
Rita, of course she contacted us, because-- I forgot to tell you that.
When she left, the family left in our attic all their prayer books, all their things that they used, the shawl and everything.
We had even the Torah from the synagogue.
We had jewels.
We had-- if the German had found out, would not have been too funny.
But they didn't.
In the attic, yes.
Because the French apartment houses all have a cellar, and they all have an attic to put the trunks, the things you don't need.
Yeah.
But there were as many attics as there were people.
You had a key and you entered.
It was like a spare room, you could say.
Only ours.
And we put all this in, and when Rita came back, we returned everything that was hers.
And for the Torah and all returned to, to the people who came to get it.
I don't know who they were.
They were sent by the synagogue or something.
I mean, not the-- somebody from the synagogue.
So generally they take the Torah, but I suppose they had several.
It was a small one.
So we kept it upstairs.
And with-- lots of books.
They were all in Hebrew.
So when my, my first husband came, I took him to the attic.
He couldn't believe his eyes.
Such a library in, in, in Hebrew.
And then Rita came back from Switzerland, and she had a very difficult career.
But she made it very well.
She went to Israel, but she couldn't stay there.
She didn't like the, the, the spirit.
By that time, she was no longer an orthodox.
She had become more or less an agnostic.
She still is, I suppose.
She's still very Jewish through loyalty, and family, and tradition, and all that.
But she--
My?
Superb, as usual.
And sure, she went to Israel.
Then she came back.
She took care of the refugee children.
There was some sort of organization.
And that's where my husband, with my consent, gave the money that was supposed to go to my engagement ring-- that's why I still don't have any engagement ring.
And then she prepared--
He was Jewish, but not terribly Jewish.
He was Jewish enough.
And he would have probably married a Jewish woman maybe.
I don't know.
Anyway, fell in love with me, married me.
We went to see the rabbi who kicked us out.
So we married with my, my pastor, who was most willing.
And that's how it was.
And--
Rita's brother, he survived.
He survived, but not in the same way as she did.
She used her whole life to exorcise, you could say, what she had in her.
So she prepared what we call the [FRENCH], which is an extremely difficult examination, which is as form our high school teachers.
You could have 500 candidate, and you take only five if there are five jobs open.
She passed it.
And then she prepared a, a-- her doctorate, but not a university doctorate as here.
A doctorate, a state doctorate, which is 10 times more difficult.
She passed it.
And then she became a professor in two of German civilization.
And then in [FRENCH], is the seventh campus.
It's like the Sorbonne, except that it is another campus.
And that's where she had her career.
She published and she published a lot of other books, important books.
I mean, they are in France.
I cannot-- and besides, they are written in French.
Thick like that.
The women in the Third Reich, the protestantism in the Third Reich.
The-- you know, all topics like that.
And the last one, which was very badly received in France-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] that's the heat-- was about the French Vichy administration, and how they collaborated.
And she found all the records, things doing with [INAUDIBLE], you know, all the stories we have now.
No.
Her brother, he, he went into business.
He married a Jewish woman, and that didn't work out at all.
He had two little girls.
And he became a, a, a wine salesman.
I mean, not a small level.
I mean, he made money.
He sold Burgundy wine and things at a high level.
Now he's retired, but he's, he's unhappy.
No, because I am the bad fortune telling you that they died.
Except Annalise, whom I lost.
I know she married-- she remarried somebody in the show business at a low level.
She was-- I have some trace of her in England.
But she didn't turn out well.
It's-- no.
I don't know what happened.
She abandoned her daughters.
I don't know.
It's-- she was a broken girl, of course, of course.
But the Oppenheimers, they didn't come back.
Beatrice, she didn't come back.
What?
Yes, I looked for them, but I haven't found their list.
I mean, I did look.
I should go to Washington.
I should do that.
I didn't do it.
I-- I don't know.
Maybe I just as well not know.
I don't know.
But they have-- they are dead.
That I know from Rita, who knows those things.
Only one-- I have to say, the French Jews I knew, they, they all came back.
I mean, they were not arrested.
They had friends in France.
They had second houses.
They had contacts.
The French, I don't say they all escaped, but the French Jews I knew all came back.
From Dijon.
And in-- one in particular-- and I shouldn't even say that.
I shouldn't even name her.
She was beautiful.
And her parents were taking good care of her.
High luxury, beauty salon, everything.
Very rich.
And when she came back, I jumped.
I said, Monique, you are back.
Oh, she said, we had such a hard time.
We didn't even have hot water.
And me, I spent my whole war without hot water.
That's one of the thing I thank God every day for my hot water when I take my shower.
She had been so spoiled, so stinking spoiled that she turned me off.
You know, she had no understanding that she let on.
Maybe she did.
But that's what she let.
She didn't want us to have pity on her.
And, you know, that kind of bothered me.
But otherwise, the other Jews I knew came back.
Simon Levi came back.
[INAUDIBLE] came back.
I mean, yes, they came back.
Well, I don't know.
I left for the States then.
Yes.
I was fed up with the whole business of Europe.
Didn't want to hear of it.
No, because you see, their properties, I think of the properties of the Tradelius or the Oppenheimer, they were sold piecemeal, you know.
They sold one table.
They sold one chest.
I mean, to, to survive, day by day.
So it was dispersed.
You could not say that somebody bought the whole thing.
They sold one coat.
They sold one fur coat, you know, one by one, depending on the needs.
No.
People didn't like to talk about it.
But for the Oppenheimer, I forgot to tell you that again with my bike, when I was at my grandparents, I went to see them in the-- in the Alps.
I was the only visitor they, they had.
And they lived in something like a barn, you know.
It still had hay in the back.
And Mrs.
Oppenheimer looked like the typical German hausfrau.
She had blonde hair in waves in a bun.
